original_abstract:
While the core objective of any 'omics science is the comprehensive measurement of all biological constituents, achieving such breadth in metabolomics is uniquely difficult. The metabolome consists of a chemically diverse array of small molecules with vastly different physical properties, ranging from highly polar sugar phosphates to hydrophobic lipids. Traditional profiling methods often introduce a distinct bias: reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) excels at capturing lipophilic compounds but fails to retain water-soluble ones, while hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) reverses this limitation.

In this study, we developed an integrative workflow that bridges these gaps by combining RPLC/MS and HILIC/MS profiling from a single biological extract. We first optimized an aminopropyl-based HILIC method by evaluating various mobile-phase additives and pH levels to ensure high sensitivity and technical reproducibility. When applied to perturbed bacterial metabolomes, this HILIC approach accurately captured key dysregulated metabolites in central carbon pathways—such as amino acids, organic acids, and phosphorylated sugars—that were otherwise lost in the RPLC void volume.

To demonstrate the utility of this dual-separation approach, we analyzed bacterial cells, human plasma, and cancer cells. Using a single extraction protocol (80% organic solvent), we successfully detected over 30,000 features per sample type, spanning both lipid and central carbon metabolism. Our data reveals that RPLC provides the highest unique feature coverage in ESI positive mode, whereas HILIC contributes the most unique information in ESI negative mode. We conclude that when sample volume and time are constrained, the most informative "snapshot" of the metabolome is achieved by pairing RPLC (ESI+) with HILIC (ESI-).
humanized_abstract:
While the primary aim of all ‘omics’ sciences is to measure the entire complement of biological molecules, metabolomics is rather unique in that it is much more difficult to obtain a truly global view. The metabolome is made up of a chemically diverse class of small molecules with a wide range of physical properties, including extremes of polarity and hydrophobicity (from polar sugar phosphates to non-polar lipids) and a corresponding wide range of molecular masses. While many current metabolomics approaches introduce some degree of selectivity, there are generally two sources of bias that are inherent to the approaches used for separation of samples for mass spectrometry analysis. So, while reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) affords excellent retention of lipophilic molecules, it does not retain highly polar species, while hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) is a reversal of this selectivity.

To address the limitations, an integrated workflow that integrates both RPLC/MS and HILIC/MS analyses from the same metabolic extract was established. An aminopropyl-based HILIC method was optimized with various salts and pHs and was applied to perturbed bacterial metabolomes. Using this optimized HILIC approach, metabolites from central carbon metabolic pathways such as amino acids, organic acids, and phosphorylated sugars that were not detectable in RPLC eluted in the void volume of the column were successfully identified.

Proof of principle was obtained by using bacteria, human plasma and mouse cancer cells as biological samples. The same extracts (80% MeOH), prepared by a single common procedure, afforded mass spectra containing more than 30,000 features, derived from lipids and central carbon metabolism, for all three biological samples. A detailed evaluation showed that, for bacteria and human plasma, the largest number of unique features was provided by RPLC in the ESI+ mode, whereas the largest number of unique features derived from HILIC was obtained in the ESI- mode. Therefore, when samples are limited in amount, time is a crucial variable and, in the conditions here considered, the best possible “metabolic snapshot” can be afforded by combining RPLC/ESI+ with HILIC/ESI-.

document.input:
While the core objective of any 'omics science is the comprehensive measurement of all biological constituents, achieving such breadth in metabolomics is uniquely difficult. The metabolome consists of a chemically diverse array of small molecules with vastly different physical properties, ranging from highly polar sugar phosphates to hydrophobic lipids. To demonstrate the utility of this dual-separation approach, we analyzed bacterial cells, human plasma, and cancer cells. We conclude that when sample volume and time are constrained, the most informative "snapshot" of the metabolome is achieved by pairing RPLC (ESI+) with HILIC (ESI-).
